profile.behavior: Forages deliberately along branches, bark, needles, and pine cones; also takes insects, spiders, fruits, and seeds, and may visit elevated feeders for seeds or suet.
field_marks: Hefty, long-tailed warbler with a relatively stout, pointed bill.
Yellow to yellow-olive head, throat, and breast; females and immatures may be much duller or grayish.
Olive-green upperparts with grayer, darker wings.
Two pale white wingbars are usually visible on the folded wing.
Pale, often broken eyering gives the face a weakly spectacled look; tail tip may show a slight central notch.
profile.habitat: Primarily pine woodland and mixed pine–deciduous forest; usually forages in pines, from the ground to the canopy, and also uses wooded edges and similar tree cover outside the breeding season.

sources.title: Pine Warbler Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Pine Warbler Life History — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: Identity, scientific name, and family are correct for Pine Warbler (Setophaga pinus, Parulidae).
The illustration correctly shows one head, one stout pointed bill, two legs, two wings (one folded/visible and one occluded by the side-on pose), and one long tail.
Plumage and structure match an adult male Pine Warbler: yellow throat/breast, yellow-olive upperparts, gray-dark folded wing with two pale wingbars, pale broken eyering, sturdy bill, and long tail.

All visible claims are supported by the live sources, including length and weight. Cornell matches the printed 19–23 cm wingspan; Audubon gives a slightly broader 20–24 cm range, so the printed range remains supported.
The habitat, behavior, feeder, palette, and field-mark text are consistent with the sources and supplied reference photos, including gray/duller female or immature plumages.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears on the candidate plate.
